parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – GHG Emissions Assessment

parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Transportation: Electric vehicle readiness ordinance
policy_commitment: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
source_quote: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure. The most cost-effective time to build charging stations and provide the electrical infrastructure for future charging stations is during initial site development. Requiring new construction to build charging stations and provide the electrical wiring needed for future stations will increase the adoption of electric vehicles and save substantial costs for future charging station installation.
validation_reasoning: The policy specifies a binding mechanism (“pass a new … ordinance”) with a clear compliance scope (new construction or renovation of parking structures) and a timeline (“in 2018”). It is spatially scoped to the jurisdiction adopting the ordinance (implied city-wide). While it lacks a numeric target, it mandates a technological/infrastructure shift (EV-ready wiring/charging infrastructure in new/renovated parking structures). These elements satisfy the criteria for actionability via binding mechanism, timeline, spatial specificity, and concrete requirement.

parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Buildings: Tune Ups
policy_commitment: Adopt Building Tune-Ups Ordinance requiring large commercial buildings to identify and implement energy and water efficiency improvements with phased compliance.
source_quote: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019. A Qualified Tune-Ups Specialist is required to complete the assessment, report to the City, and monitor implementation of operational and maintenance improvements. Examples of operational fixes include changes to thermostat set points, or adjusting lighting or irrigation schedules. Tune-ups also review HVAC, lighting, and water systems to identify needed maintenance, cleaning or repairs. These types of improvements typically reduce individual building energy use an estimated 10-15 percent. Across the entire commercial building sector, the tune-up mandate is expected to reduce energy use 5-8 percent and GHG emissions by 6-9 percent.

has_geographic_scope: Yes — buildings 50,000 sq ft and larger

While the percent reductions (10–15% per building; 5–8% sector energy; 6–9% GHG) are estimates rather than mandated targets, the ordinance’s enforceable requirements, compliance deadlines, and reporting make it actionable and monitorable. The size threshold and explicit processes provide measurable coverage, and the legal mechanism compensates for any lack of a binding numeric performance target.

parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Buildings: Tune-Up Accelerator
policy_commitment: Implement the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program to incentivize early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance for mid-size buildings.
source_quote: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance. The Accelerator is a voluntary program for owners of mid-size buildings, those 50,000-100,000 square feet, to conduct a tune-up that meets the Seattle Building Tune-Up requirements in advance of when their building is required to comply (2020 & 2021). Building owners can receive Seattle City Light incentives for the tune-up and will be encouraged to go beyond a tune-up by conducting additional energy conservation measures. The University of Washington Integrated Design Lab is providing no-cost technical support and guidance for owners pursing more extensive energy upgrades. Participating energy service providers will perform building assessments and assist owners in meeting the tune-up requirements, as well as collect building characteristic data and work with owners to pursue additional energy-savings opportunities.
validation_reasoning:
The policy is actionable due to:
